_readme: Curated 'commonly confused' pair index. Each pair is A vs B with a one-line distinguisher. featureId links into the map via map.html?feature=<id>; leave null if the counterpart isn't in the atlas.

confusions.key: Aravalli runs SW–NE INSIDE Rajasthan; Vindhya runs E–W SOUTH of Rajasthan.

left.name: Aravalli Range
left.distinguisher: ~800 Ma fold mountains; runs SW-NE through Rajasthan; climatic divide (Thar west, semi-arid east); Guru Shikhar is highest peak.

right.name: Vindhya Range
right.distinguisher: E-W trend on the Malwa plateau margin, SOUTH of Rajasthan; separates North Indian plain from Deccan; the Kota-Bundi plateau is its northern edge.

confusions.key: Chambal enters Rajasthan from MP (south); Banas is an entirely intra-Rajasthan river draining Mewar into Chambal.

left.name: Chambal River
left.distinguisher: 1,024 km — longest river in Rajasthan; rises in Janapav (MP), forms the Kota-Baran Chambal cascade with Gandhi Sagar, Rana Pratap Sagar, Jawahar Sagar, Kota Barrage.

right.name: Banas River
right.distinguisher: Longest INTRA-Rajasthan river; rises in Khamnor (Rajsamand), flows through Bhilwara-Tonk and joins Chambal at Rameshwar Ghat; Bisalpur dam is on the Banas.

confusions.key: Luni ends in the Rann of Kutch (a saline swamp); Ghaggar simply disappears into the desert with NO outlet.

left.name: Luni River
left.distinguisher: Rises in the Aravalli near Ajmer (Naga hills); Rajasthan's principal Marwar drainage; 495 km; terminates in the Rann of Kutch (Gujarat). Its water turns saline downstream of Balotra.

right.name: Ghaggar River
right.distinguisher: The 'lost' Saraswati candidate; enters at Hanumangarh, dies out in the Thar without reaching the sea; associated with the Kalibangan Harappan site.

confusions.key: Sambhar is India's largest inland salt lake AND a Ramsar site; Pachpadra is smaller but the source of Sambhar-quality 'Punjab salt' historically.

left.name: Sambhar Lake
left.distinguisher: Rajasthan's LARGEST salt lake; ~9% of India's salt output; Ramsar site (1990); on the Nagaur-Jaipur boundary.

right.name: Pachpadra Lake
right.distinguisher: Smaller salt lake in Barmer; unique for the twig-based crystallisation method ('lana khar'). No Ramsar status. Sits above the Barmer petroleum basin.

confusions.key: Ranthambhore NP is inside Ranthambhore Tiger Reserve — the TR is larger (adds Sawai Man Singh WLS + Kailadevi WLS).

left.name: Ranthambhore NP
left.distinguisher: Notified 1980, ~392 km²; the CORE zone; sits within the tiger reserve.

right.name: Ranthambhore TR
right.distinguisher: Notified 1973; ~1411 km² total (NP + Sawai Man Singh WLS + Kailadevi WLS); one of India's first 9 Project Tiger reserves.

confusions.key: Keoladeo (Bharatpur) is a UNESCO World Heritage bird sanctuary; Sultanpur (Haryana) is a smaller NP without WHS status.

left.name: Keoladeo Ghana NP
left.distinguisher: UNESCO WHS (1985); Ramsar site (1981); man-made 18th-c. Bharatpur royal duck-shoot converted into India's premier bird sanctuary.

right.name: Sultanpur NP
right.distinguisher: In Haryana (Gurgaon) — NOT Rajasthan; also famous for migratory birds, but neither a WHS nor a Ramsar site of the same scale.

confusions.key: Desert NP is in Rajasthan (Jaisalmer-Barmer); Kutch Desert WS is in Gujarat.

left.name: Desert National Park
left.distinguisher: Rajasthan's largest NP (~3162 km²); in Jaisalmer-Barmer; the last stronghold of the Godawan (Great Indian Bustard).

right.name: Kutch Desert WS
right.distinguisher: In Gujarat's Rann of Kutch; famous for wild ass (Ghudkhur) and flamingoes. Different biome (salt marsh, not sand-desert).

confusions.key: Godawan = Great Indian Bustard (Rajasthan's state bird); Sonchiraiya = Lesser Florican, a smaller bustard.

left.name: Godawan (Great Indian Bustard)
left.distinguisher: Rajasthan's state bird (1981); critically endangered; stronghold in Desert NP; ~150 birds left in India.

right.name: Sonchiraiya (Lesser Florican)
right.distinguisher: Smaller bustard, endangered; monsoon breeding visitor to Shokaliya (Ajmer); NOT the state bird — often confused because both are grassland bustards.

confusions.key: Khejri is the state TREE; Rohira is the state FLOWER (and also a tree — the desert teak).

left.name: Khejri
left.distinguisher: State tree (1983). Prosopis cineraria. Sacred to the Bishnoi; the 1730 Khejarli sacrifice defended it.

right.name: Rohira
right.distinguisher: State FLOWER (not tree, 1983). Tecomella undulata. Called 'Marwar teak'; golden-yellow flowers; native to Aravalli-Thar.

confusions.key: Chittorgarh saw three great jauhars; each by a different queen against a different attacker.

left.name: 1303 Padmini Jauhar
left.distinguisher: Rani Padmini; attacker Alauddin Khilji (Delhi Sultanate); Rawal Ratan Singh's queen.

right.name: 1535 & 1568 Jauhars
right.distinguisher: 1535: Rani Karnavati vs Bahadur Shah of Gujarat. 1568: 'Sakka' vs Akbar (final jauhar under Rani Phool Kanwar); after this, Chittor never rebuilt as capital.

confusions.key: Khanwa (1527) was Rana Sanga's DEFEAT by Babur; Haldighati (1576) was Maharana Pratap's indecisive stand against Akbar's Man Singh.

left.name: Battle of Khanwa (1527)
left.distinguisher: Rana Sanga of Mewar vs Babur; decisive Mughal victory that consolidated Babur's Indian conquest. Site: Khanwa near Bharatpur.

right.name: Battle of Haldighati (1576)
right.distinguisher: Maharana Pratap vs Akbar's forces led by Raja Man Singh I of Amber; tactically inconclusive; Pratap escaped on Chetak; site: Rakt Talai near Nathdwara.

confusions.key: Both were Mewar capitals — but Kumbhalgarh was NEVER breached in war; Chittor fell three times (jauhars).

left.name: Kumbhalgarh Fort
left.distinguisher: Built by Rana Kumbha (r. 1433-68); 36-km walls — world's 2nd longest continuous wall; birthplace of Maharana Pratap; NEVER breached (fell only once via water-poisoning trick, 1578).

right.name: Chittorgarh Fort
right.distinguisher: India's largest fort by area (280 ha); Mewar CAPITAL until 1568; site of the three great jauhars; abandoned as capital after 1568 Akbar siege.

confusions.key: All three ring Jaipur — Amber is the old CAPITAL (pre-1727); Jaigarh has the cannon foundry; Nahargarh is the northern watchtower.

left.name: Amber (Amer) Fort
left.distinguisher: Kachhawaha CAPITAL from 967 CE to 1727; UNESCO Hill Fort; the Sheesh Mahal is here; built up by Raja Man Singh I (1592).

right.name: Jaigarh + Nahargarh
right.distinguisher: Jaigarh (1726): direct above Amber, houses 'Jaivan' — the world's largest wheeled cannon; had India's only in-fort cannon foundry. Nahargarh (1734): northern watchtower; ring-fort trio with Amber and Jaigarh.

confusions.key: Three gold-based crafts — all different: Thewa is gold-on-glass (Pratapgarh); Meenakari is enamel-on-gold (Jaipur); Kundan is uncut-stone gem-setting (Jaipur).

left.name: Thewa (Pratapgarh)
left.distinguisher: Openwork gold sheet fused onto molten COLOURED GLASS at 500°C. Only in Pratapgarh; only Raj-Soni families make it.

right.name: Meenakari + Kundan (Jaipur)
right.distinguisher: Meenakari = enamel (mineral oxides fired on gold, no glass). Kundan = 24-carat gold gem-setting with uncut precious stones. Both practised together in Jaipur's Johari Bazaar.

confusions.key: Bandhej is TIE-DYE (resist); Bagru/Sanganer are BLOCK-PRINTS (applied dye). Different technique family.

left.name: Bandhej & Leheriya
left.distinguisher: TIE-DYE — thousands of knots tied before dyeing (Bandhej dots) or diagonal rolling (Leheriya waves). Shekhawati and Jaipur belt.

right.name: Bagru & Sanganer
right.distinguisher: HAND-BLOCK PRINTS — carved wooden blocks stamp dye onto cloth. Sanganer = fine floral; Bagru = earth-tone geometric with iron-mordant dyes.

confusions.key: Both are block-print villages 30 km apart — but Sanganeri uses fine floral vegetable dyes; Bagru uses earth-tone iron-mordant.

left.name: Sanganeri Printing
left.distinguisher: Fine floral motifs on cotton; VEGETABLE dyes; light palette (pastel red, indigo); practised by the Chhipa community.

right.name: Bagru Printing
right.distinguisher: Earth-tone geometric patterns; IRON-MORDANT dyes; darker palette (indigo, ochre, madder); black lines from ferrous mud.

confusions.key: Both are metal-work embroidery of Rajasthan; Danka uses SQUARE leaf-pieces (Udaipur); Gota-Patti uses ribbon STRIPS (Ajmer/Jaipur).

left.name: Danka (Udaipur)
left.distinguisher: Tiny SQUARE dankas of gold/silver leaf hand-stitched onto silk. Only ~20 master families; Mewar royalty commission.

right.name: Gota-Patti (Jaipur/Ajmer)
right.distinguisher: Continuous thin metal RIBBONS applied as appliqué. Cheaper, more widespread; hallmark of Jaipuri bridal outfits.

confusions.key: Pichwai hangs BEHIND a temple deity (small ritual cloth); Phad is a LONG NARRATIVE scroll for wandering priests.

left.name: Pichwai (Nathdwara)
left.distinguisher: Devotional cloth painting placed BEHIND the Shrinathji idol; 24 seasonal rotations; deep blacks/greens/gold; Adi-Gaur painter families.

right.name: Phad (Bhilwara)
right.distinguisher: 20-30 ft long horizontal narrative scroll; unrolled by wandering Bhopa priests as they sing folk-hero epics (Pabuji, Devnarayan). Practised in Bhilwara-Shahpura.

confusions.key: Sister schools that split in 1631 — Bundi favoured baramasa and rasikapriya; Kota specialised in JUNGLE HUNTS.

left.name: Bundi School
left.distinguisher: Palatial night skies, monsoon-clouds, round faces with fish-eyes; Baramasa/Ragamala themes; Chitrashala within Taragarh Fort.

right.name: Kota School
right.distinguisher: Over-life-size TIGER AND BOAR HUNTS; dense entangled forest; action-drawing at a scale unusual for miniature painting. Peaked under Umed Singh I.

confusions.key: Both are Mughal-influenced BUT — Kishangarh gave us Bani-Thani (elongated faces, Krishna themes); Bikaner is the most technically REFINED and Sufi-linked.

left.name: Kishangarh School
left.distinguisher: 'Bani-Thani' style — elongated faces, half-closed almond eyes; developed under Sawant Singh (Nagari Das); painter Nihal Chand. Radha-Krishna themes.

right.name: Bikaner School
right.distinguisher: Pale ivory tones, Persian palette (mauve, silver, sap-green); painters SIGNED and dated (rare); Usta-Muslim community; Ali Raza, Ruknuddin masters.

confusions.key: Ghoomar is the STATE dance (Rajput women); Kalbeliya is the UNESCO-listed serpent dance of the Kalbelia gypsy community.

left.name: Ghoomar
left.distinguisher: Bhil-origin women's whirl dance adopted by Rajputs; STATE DANCE; concentric circles around a central lamp; hallmark of Teej and weddings.

right.name: Kalbeliya
right.distinguisher: UNESCO Intangible Heritage (2010); serpent-inspired sinuous movement; performed by the Kalbelia snake-charmer community; Gulabo Sapera brought it to world attention.

confusions.key: Marwar = the JODHPUR cultural region (Rathore heartland). Merwara = the British-era ADMINISTRATIVE province around Ajmer (a Chief-Commissioner's Province till 1956).

left.name: Marwar (cultural region)
left.distinguisher: The classical Rathore homeland — Jodhpur, Pali, Nagaur, Barmer, Jalore, Jaisalmer. A CULTURAL region.

right.name: Ajmer-Merwara (colonial unit)
right.distinguisher: 1818-1956 British Chief-Commissioner's Province around Ajmer; NOT a princely state; joined Rajasthan by 1956 States Reorganisation, not by the 1948-50 integration.

confusions.key: Both are cultural regions; Matsya is the NORTHEAST (Alwar-Bharatpur), Vagad is the SOUTHERN tribal belt (Banswara-Dungarpur).

left.name: Matsya region
left.distinguisher: Northeast Rajasthan — Alwar, Bharatpur, Dholpur, Karauli; Mahabharata-era name; briefly a merged state (18 Mar 1948-15 May 1949) before joining Rajasthan Union.

right.name: Vagad region
right.distinguisher: Southern tribal belt — Banswara, Dungarpur, southern Pratapgarh; Bhil-dominated; TSP Fifth-Schedule Area; Mahi Bajaj Sagar irrigates the belt.

confusions.key: Chinkara is the STATE animal (a small gazelle); Blackbuck is a separate antelope with spiral horns.

left.name: Chinkara
left.distinguisher: State animal (1981) — the Indian gazelle, small (~25 kg), pale-tan coat, straight horns. Ranges across Thar, Sariska, Ranthambhore.

right.name: Blackbuck
right.distinguisher: Larger (~40 kg) antelope; males turn black-brown with SPIRAL horns; state animal of neighbouring Haryana/Punjab. Tal Chhapar Sanctuary (Churu) is Rajasthan's key blackbuck reserve.

confusions.key: Kalibangan is a HARAPPAN site on the Ghaggar (northwest Rajasthan); Ahar (Aharh) is a CHALCOLITHIC pre-Harappan culture on the Ayad river near Udaipur.

left.name: Kalibangan
left.distinguisher: Hanumangarh district; on the (extinct) Saraswati/Ghaggar; MATURE HARAPPAN + pre-Harappan phases; oldest ploughed field known in the world (c. 2800 BCE); world's earliest earthquake evidence.

right.name: Ahar-Banas culture
right.distinguisher: Ayad river near Udaipur; ~3000-1500 BCE; CHALCOLITHIC (copper-stone), independent of Harappa; ~90 sites in southern Rajasthan; Ahar copper artefacts predate Harappan copper.
